description: Prune english-1024-balanced-v1: remove tokens with decoded length >20 bytes and obvious multiword composites, resize down to 960 to tighten the vocabulary around atomic units

decision.headline: pruned vocab regresses BPB vs baseline
decision.reasoning: The candidate scores 4.1183 BPB versus the frontier's 4.1023, a regression of ~0.016 BPB. Shrinking to 960 tokens and pruning long/multiword tokens did not compensate for the lost vocabulary coverage. The 3.9% dead vocab fraction also suggests the pruning was not surgical enough.
decision.next_direction: try a lighter prune — keep vocab at 1024 and only delete the most egregious multi-word composites without resizing down

description: Light prune of balanced-v1: remove only space+punct tokens, keep vocab near 1024 by resizing to 1008 instead of aggressive 960

decision.headline: regression over baseline balanced-v1
decision.reasoning: The pruned candidate scores 4.1213 BPB vs the frontier 4.1023 BPB — a ~0.019 regression. While the approach is reasonable (light pruning of space+punct tokens), the 4.4% dead vocab fraction and reduced effective vocab (964 used out of 1008) suggest the removed tokens were actually useful. The simplicity gain doesn't justify the quality loss.
decision.next_direction: try minimal resize to 1016-1020 without any token deletion to isolate whether resize alone helps or hurts

description: Minimal prune: only remove very long tokens (>40 bytes) and triple-space runs, no resize — stay as close to original 1024 as possible

decision.headline: marginal gain, unnecessary complexity
decision.reasoning: The score improvement is only 0.0009 BPB over the baseline (4.1014 vs 4.1023), well within the baseline's std of 0.145. The pruning introduces complexity (regex deletion, resize to 1020) for a statistically insignificant gain. The 4.2% dead vocab fraction also suggests the pruning didn't meaningfully improve token utilization. We should prefer the clean original vocabulary.
decision.next_direction: Try a different TokenMonster family member (e.g. english-1024-consistent-v1 or english-1024-clean-v1) rather than pruning the balanced variant

description: Minimal pruning from balanced-v1: keep 1022 tokens, only remove extremely long tokens (50+ bytes) and runs of 5+ spaces, preserving nearly all original vocabulary

decision.headline: No improvement over baseline
decision.reasoning: The candidate scores 4.1024 vs the baseline 4.1023 — essentially identical or marginally worse. Pruning 2 tokens and removing long tokens/space runs added complexity without any measurable gain. The 4.4% dead vocab fraction also suggests the pruning didn't meaningfully improve token utilization.
decision.next_direction: Try modifying token composition rather than just pruning: explore strict/clean/consistent variants of the 1024 balanced family, or test other TokenMonster 1024 prebuilts directly

description: Minimal pruning: keep size at 1024, only prune tokens with decoded length >45 bytes and very long whitespace runs

decision.headline: no improvement over baseline
decision.reasoning: The candidate scores 4.1033 vs the frontier 4.1023, which is marginally worse. The pruning removed very little (977/1024 tokens used, 4.6% dead vocab) and the bytes_per_token of 2.45 is essentially unchanged. There is no meaningful signal that this direction helps.
decision.next_direction: try more aggressive pruning of low-frequency or redundant tokens and backfill with single-byte or short tokens to improve coverage

description: Minimal pruning from balanced-v1: resize 1018, cap decoded at 35 bytes, delete only extreme whitespace runs

decision.headline: minimal pruning beats balanced-v1 baseline
decision.reasoning: Score 4.1005 vs frontier 4.1023 is a small but real improvement (~0.002 BPB). The approach is conservative: only 6 tokens removed via resize, mild byte cap, and deleting only extreme whitespace runs. Dead vocab fraction of 4% is acceptable. Vocab size 1018 stays near the 1024 target.
decision.next_direction: try the same minimal pruning but with resize 1020-1022 to see if keeping a few more tokens improves further

description: match exp6 pruning (max_decoded_bytes=35, regex 4+) but resize to 1020 instead of 1018 to test if two extra tokens help

decision.headline: resize to 1020 slightly worse than 1018
decision.reasoning: Score 4.1014 vs frontier 4.1005 — the two extra tokens did not help and slightly hurt. The unique tokens used (977) and tokens_per_byte are identical, meaning the extra slots just increased dead vocab fraction from 4.0% to 4.2% with no encoding benefit. No reason to prefer this over exp6.
decision.next_direction: try resize 1016 or 1014 to see if trimming further below 1018 improves score by reducing dead vocab

description: consistent-v1 base with the winning light-pruning recipe from exp45 at resize 1018

decision.headline: consistent-v1 base at 1018 scores 4.090 vs frontier 4.084, no improvement
decision.reasoning: Experiment 0048 scores 4.090, which is worse than the current frontier of 4.084 from experiment 0045. Both target vocab_size 1018, but this uses the consistent-v1 base instead of whatever base produced 0045. The consistent-v1 base yields slightly worse tokens_per_byte (0.4074 vs 0.4054) and lower bytes_per_token (2.455 vs 2.466), confirming 0045's base is better at this size. Dead vocab fraction improved (0.029 vs 0.039) but that didn't translate to a better score.
decision.next_direction: try the original exp45 recipe at vocab sizes 1020 and 1022 to find the sweet spot, as the frontier's next_direction already suggests

description: clean-v1 resize 1014 with minimal pruning — continuing downward from best at 1018, testing if further modest reduction improves proxy score

decision.headline: resize 1014 edges out prior best 4.084 → 4.082
decision.reasoning: Score improved from 4.084 to 4.082, a small but real gain. The candidate uses the same clean-v1 base with minimal pruning, just slightly lower vocab size. Dead vocab fraction decreased from 3.9% to 3.6%, indicating tighter utilization. tokens_per_byte and bytes_per_token are unchanged, so the compression ratio is holding steady despite the smaller vocab.
decision.next_direction: try resize 1010 and 1012 to see if the downward trend continues, and also test 1016 to bracket the optimum between 1014 and 1018

description: clean-v1 base with aggressive targeted pruning (multiword + digit-letter hybrids + rare punctuation clusters) and resize to 1010, testing whether deeper pruning with a lower resize unlocks improvement below the 1014 plateau

decision.headline: resize 1010 regresses to 4.090 vs frontier 4.082
decision.reasoning: The score of 4.090 is worse than the current frontier of 4.082 at resize 1014. The aggressive targeted pruning with additional delete_regex patterns and lower resize did not help — it increased tokens_per_byte (0.4065 vs 0.4054) and raised dead_vocab_frac (3.96% vs 3.55%). This suggests 1014 is closer to the optimum and going lower hurts.
decision.next_direction: try resize 1012 to narrow the bracket between 1010 (bad) and 1014 (best), and also test 1016 to confirm the optimum neighborhood

description: balanced-v1 base with targeted space-punctuation composite pruning and no resize, testing whether removing awkward space+punct fragments improves balanced vocab without downsizing

decision.headline: space-punct pruning regresses score 4.082 → 4.103
decision.reasoning: The candidate scores 4.103, well below the frontier best of 4.082 from experiment 0049 (resize 1014). The space-punctuation pruning strategy increased dead vocab fraction to 4.6% while worsening tokens-per-byte, indicating the deleted tokens were actually useful. No improvement on any metric.
decision.next_direction: follow the frontier's next_direction: try resize 1010 and 1012 to see if the downward trend from 1014 continues, and test 1016 to bracket the optimum

description: consistent-v1 base with aggressive multiword and composite pruning, no resize — structurally different from the clean-v1 resize hill-climb that dominated recent experiments

decision.headline: consistent-v1 base scores 4.093, worse than frontier 4.082
decision.reasoning: The consistent-v1 base with aggressive pruning scored 4.093, which is worse than the current frontier of 4.082 from experiment 0049 (clean-v1 resize 1014). The structural divergence from the clean-v1 line did not pay off. The consistent-v1 base appears to be a weaker starting point for this task despite the pruning strategy being reasonable.
decision.next_direction: continue the clean-v1 resize hill-climb: test resize 1010 and 1012 to see if the downward trend from 1018→1014 continues, and bracket the optimum

description: clean-v1 base, no resize, aggressive targeted pruning of digit-heavy, repeated-char, and rare-punctuation tokens to improve vocabulary quality without shrinking size

decision.headline: pruning regressed score 4.082 → 4.086, higher dead vocab
decision.reasoning: Experiment 0053 scores 4.086 vs the frontier 4.082, a clear regression. The aggressive regex-based pruning did not improve compression and actually increased dead vocab fraction from 3.6% to 4.5%. The resize-1014 approach in experiment 0049 remains superior.
decision.next_direction: continue bracketing the resize optimum: try resize 1010 and 1012 to see if the downward trend from 1014 continues, and test 1016 to find the exact sweet spot

description: clean-v1 aggressive downsize to 998 with minimal pruning — test whether the improvement trend from 1024→1014 continues further below the explored band

decision.headline: resize 998 beats prior best 4.082 → 4.074, downward trend continues
decision.reasoning: Score improved from 4.082 to 4.074, continuing the downward trend from 1024→1018→1014→998. Dead vocab fraction dropped to 2.0% which is healthy. tokens_per_byte and bytes_per_token are identical to the prior best, suggesting the resize is trimming only dead weight without hurting coverage. The candidate stays within the clean-v1 family with minimal parameter changes.
decision.next_direction: bracket the optimum: try resize 990 and 1006 to find where the improvement plateaus between 998 and 1014

description: Apply winning resize 998 to balanced-v1 base (best unpruned baseline) with same minimal pruning as frontier exp 54 — tests whether resize benefit transfers across base vocabs

decision.headline: balanced-v1 base at resize 998 scores 4.091 vs frontier 4.074 — no improvement
decision.reasoning: The balanced-v1 base vocab at resize 998 scores 4.091, which is worse than the current frontier of 4.074 from experiment 54 (also resize 998 but on the prior base). This confirms the resize benefit does not transfer equally across base vocabs — the existing base is stronger. The tokens_per_byte is also slightly worse (0.408 vs 0.405), indicating less efficient encoding.
decision.next_direction: Continue bracketing around the frontier's base vocab: try resize 990 and 1006 to find where the improvement plateaus near the 998 optimum

description: consistent-v1 base at resize 998 with targeted single-char and digit-only token pruning — tests whether the winning resize point transfers to a different base vocab with qualitatively different deletions

decision.headline: consistent-v1 base regresses to 4.108 vs frontier 4.074
decision.reasoning: The consistent-v1 base vocab at the same resize point (998) scores 4.108, which is 0.034 worse than the frontier's 4.074 on the default english-1024 base. The higher tokens_per_byte (0.411 vs 0.405) and more dead vocab (2.5% vs 2.0%) confirm this base produces a less efficient encoding. No evidence of stagnation on the current base to justify switching.
decision.next_direction: stay on english-1024 base and bracket the optimum: try resize 990 and 1006 to find where improvement plateaus around the 998 sweet spot

description: clean-v1 resize 980 to test if downward trend continues below best-scoring 998, same minimal pruning regime that produced the frontier

decision.headline: resize 980 regresses to 4.085, confirming 998 is near the optimum floor
decision.reasoning: Score of 4.085 is worse than the frontier's 4.074 at resize 998, reversing the downward trend. This establishes a lower bound — going below 998 hurts compression efficiency more than the vocab reduction helps. The bytes_per_token dropped from 2.466 to 2.454, meaning tokens got shorter on average, which increases total token count and worsens the score.
decision.next_direction: try resize 1006 to bracket the optimum from above and confirm 998 is the sweet spot in the 990-1014 range

description: clean-v1 at winning resize 998 with aggressive multiword+space-punct pruning — materially different pruning regime at the proven sweet spot

decision.headline: clean-v1 base regresses vs consistent-v1 at same resize 998
decision.reasoning: Score 4.093 is worse than the frontier 4.074 at the same vocab size 998. The clean-v1 base with aggressive pruning produces higher dead_vocab_frac (3.8% vs 2.0%) and worse tokens_per_byte, indicating the pruning regime removes useful tokens. The consistent-v1 base remains clearly superior at this resize point.
decision.next_direction: bracket the optimum around resize 998 on consistent-v1: try resize 990 and 1006 to find where improvement plateaus
